What are the key features of the Shakti EW System?

The Shakti EW System consists of four RFPSs and one Radar Data Processor (RDP). Four RFPSs provide ES coverage at frequencies ranging from 0.175-40 GHz, while two additional RFPSs extend EA coverage to 6-40 GHz. The RDP receives data from the RFPSs via RF links and transmits it to the RFPSs for analysis and display on the console or Emitter Library.

what are the technical specifications of signal types for shakti EA and ES segment?

The technical specifications for signal types in the Shakti EA and ES segments include various frequency types such as Pulse, CW, Frequency Agile (comprising Random, Periodic, Ramp & Fix), Agile Batch (up to 16 values with 8-512 pulses per batch), FMCW, Chirp, Phase coded, and Phase modulated signals. The Pulse Repetition Interval (PRI) types supported are Fixed, Jitter (30%), Staggered (16 levels), Dwell & Switch (up to 16 values with 10 to 100 msec dwell time), Sliding PRI, and Wobulated PRI. Additionally, the system supports Pulse Compression and Amplitude modulated signals.

Step-by-step explanation of the operation of the Radar Finger Printing System (RFPS).

1. Receives RF signals from radars at frequencies between 0.175 and 40 GHz.
2. Analyzes each pulse using Fine Grain Inter-Pulse Analysis (FGIPA) and Fine Grained Intra-Pulse Analysis (FGIPA).
3. Identifies the specific emitter by comparing fingerprints to known radars and their characteristics.
4. Generates reports containing detailed fingerprint results for each emitter detected.

This information is then compared against stored patterns and alarms are raised when matches are found.

Explain the Step-by-Step process for detecting Radar Emissions accurately using Probability of Intercept (POI).

1. A signal from the Radar Emitter is generated by the ES Processor.
2. This signal is converted to a digital format and sent to the RFPS processor.
3. The RFPS processor converts the digital signal into a frequency-domain representation.
4. Based on the received signal strength and characteristics, the RFPS software classifies the radar emitter as being active or passive.
5. If the emitter is classified as active, the RFPS software calculates the probability of intercept (POI) based on parameters such as frequency, phase, amplitude, etc.
6. Using the calculated POI, the RFPS software determines whether the radar emitter is detected successfully or not.
7. Finally, the result is displayed on the monitor, and if successful detection is confirmed, the 'Detected' message is shown and the status is updated in the database.

Step-by-step explanation of how the Mission Library stores radar entries.

1. The Mission Library has a storage capacity of up to 20,000 radar entries.
2. These entries are stored on a hard drive that consists of 419,360 sectors (each sector holds 768 bytes).
3. Each sector is divided into 16 sectors each containing 32KB. These are organized by threat type and frequency band. In addition, each sector contains an index file for quick access.
4. Prior to entry insertion, the system checks whether the sector is full or not. If it is empty, the entry is inserted; otherwise, the entry is placed at the end of the sector and marked as "Full".
5. To prevent overloading, the system uses a first-in-first-out (FIFO) algorithm for inserting entries. Entries are extracted from the front of the queue until they reach their destination location.

What are the step-by-step details for using the Shakti ES system?

1. Ensure that both RFPS and RFUE systems are powered on.
2. Connect the RFPS system to the ESM unit through the RS-485 interface.
3. Connect the RFUE system to the ESM unit through the RS-485 interface.
4. Power up the RFPS system and connect the RFPS to the ESM via the RS-485 interface.
5. Power up the RFUE system and connect it to the ESM via the RS-485 interface.
6. Power up the RFPS and RFUE systems and verify that they are operational.
7. Configure the ESM parameters such as baud rate, power supply, and display settings.
8. Set up IP address and DNS server details to enable internet connectivity.
9. Run the ESM application to initialize the system and load configuration files.
10. Start the ESM application and monitor the system health status in real-time.
11. Use the RFPS and RFUE applications to receive signals from the RFPS and RFUE sensors.
12. Analyze signal data and display results through the ESM application GUI.
13. Save and export data to file for offline analysis or review.
14. Shutdown the system by stopping the ESM application and resetting the RFPS and RFUE units before switching off the ESM unit.

Cover Pulse: A short pulse after detection confirms the identification of the radar before entering its jamming phase.

What is the procedure for switching on Shakti system?

To switch on the Shakti system: (a) Ensure all Racks are in remote operate mode and MCBs for all Racks & UPS are ON. (b) Switch ON the MCB on the console desk side and verify 'Mains ON' and '24V' LEDs are glowing. (c) Press 'SCD ON' and verify that the switch LED, Monitor-1 & 2, SCD Processor, and Ethernet SW-2 & SW-3 are powered ON. (d) Press 'ES ON' on the console desk and verify the switch indication and power to ES Rack-1, ES Rack-2, ES1 & Ethernet switch-1, as well as the health status indicators. (e) Press 'EA ON' and observe that the switch LED glows and EA Rack-1, EA Rack-2, MMJ AC/DC units, MBJ AC/DC units, and LRU are powered ON sequentially. (f) After SCD is powered ON, individual ES and EA subunits can be powered. (g) Press 'ESM ON', verify the LED glows, and check that 24VDC is routed to ES Rack-1 and ESI Unit. (h) Press 'ECM ON', verify the LED glows, and check that 24VDC is routed to EA Rack-1, HEU(P), and HEU(STBD). (j) After ECM is on, switch ON the jammer unit. (k) Press 'Jammer Init', verify the LED glows and 24VDC is routed to the Jammer Unit. Finally, the 'Shakti EW System - SCD' application will start automatically and display the User Authentication screen.

Explain step-by-step how the UPS works.

1. The UPS monitors the incoming power quality at the UPS input. If the input voltage is outside the specified range, the UPS detects it and switches to the battery bank automatically. 2. When the main switch is closed, the UPS starts to charge the battery via DC-DC converter. Once the battery voltage reaches the required level, the UPS allows the main switch to open and begins to deliver AC power to the UPS output. 3. During normal operation, the UPS continuously monitors the power quality and adjusts the switching frequency according to the priority of loads. 4. In case of load shedding, the UPS will switch to battery mode and continue to provide power to the system.

What broad functional capabilities does the RFPS provide for emitter analysis and identification?

The RFPS provides capabilities to analyze, classify, and identify conventional, exotic, and LPI radars. It performs fine-grain measurements of frequency, pulse width, phase, and PRI, and conducts intra-pulse analysis of amplitude, phase, and frequency. Additionally, it automatically detects coded and agile radars, classifies emitters with confidence measures, supports platform identification, maintains a library of 10,000 emitters, extracts features for LPI classification, stores IF data for offline analysis, auto-scans the 2.2–18 GHz range, and displays detected emitters.

Step-by-step explanation of how the RFPS analyzes emitter data.

1. The RFPS analyzes the radar's transmitted spectrum at 512 kHz resolution across the entire 2.2–18 GHz band using a dedicated processor. 2. Fine-grained measurements of frequency (±1 Hz), phase, pulse width (±40 μs), and PRI (±30 dB) are performed on each measured point. 3. A fine-grained interpulse analysis is conducted to detect any differences between pulses within the same pulse block. 4. The IF data is extracted from the scanned spectrum and stored for offline analysis. 5. Automatic detection of coded and agile radars is achieved through correlation techniques. 6. The 'Classifier Library' contains more than 10,000 predefined emitters classified according to their characteristics. 7. The Auto-Scan feature scans the entire frequency band from 2.2–18 GHz, covering all bands including S-band. 8. Confidence levels are assigned based on the precision and accuracy of the analyzed data. 9. Automatic identification reduces the need for manual intervention by identifying emitters automatically. 10. Detailed data such as frequency, amplitude, phase, pulse width, and PRI are displayed for further review.

What is the Electronic Attack (EA) System?

The Electronic Attack (EA) System operates in the 6–40 GHz frequency range using high-power jammers based on Digital RF Memory (DRFM) technology and Microwave Power Modules (MPMs) to counter modern radar threats. It is divided into two bands: 6–18 GHz and 18–40 GHz. The system is configured into two identical subsystems on the port and starboard sides of the ship, supported by a common EA processor. It can simultaneously handle up to 10 threats across 360° coverage.
